era_context: The final year of the Napoleonic Wars in Europe. Napoleon's Grande Armée is collapsing under the weight of the Sixth Coalition (Russia, Prussia, Austria, Britain, Sweden, Spain, and Portugal). The War of 1812 between the United States and Britain is ongoing. Latin American independence movements are accelerating. The Industrial Revolution is transforming Britain. The Qing Dynasty in China and the Tokugawa shogunate in Japan remain dominant in their regions.

events.title: Abdication of Napoleon; end of the Napoleonic Wars in Europe
events.description: Napoleon Bonaparte abdicated on April 11, 1814, following the military collapse of the French Empire under the pressure of the Sixth Coalition. The allied armies (Russian, Prussian, Austrian, British, Spanish, Portuguese, and Swedish) had invaded France and defeated the French army in a series of battles in early 1814. Paris fell to the allies on March 31, 1814. Napoleon was forced to abdicate and was exiled to the island of Elba in the Mediterranean. The abdication marked the end of the Napoleonic Wars in Europe and the beginning of the Congress of Vienna to reshape the European political order. Louis XVIII, the Bourbon heir, was restored to the French throne.

sources.name: Napoleon's abdication document (April 11, 1814)
Military records and eyewitness accounts from the 1814 campaign
Chandler (1966), 'The Campaigns of Napoleon'

events.certainty_note: The abdication date (April 11, 1814) is precisely documented in official records.

events.title: Treaty of Ghent signed; War of 1812 ends (ratified 1815)
events.region: North America (United States and British North America)
events.description: The Treaty of Ghent was signed on December 24, 1814, between the United States and Britain, officially ending the War of 1812. The treaty restored pre-war boundaries and did not address the issues that had prompted the war (impressment of American sailors, trade restrictions). However, the war's end allowed the United States to focus on westward expansion and economic development. The treaty was ratified by both nations in 1815. The war had been largely a stalemate, with neither side achieving its objectives, but it boosted American nationalism and confidence.
key_figures: American negotiators: John Quincy Adams, James A. Bayard, Henry Clay, Jonathan Russell, Albert Gallatin

sources.name: Treaty of Ghent (December 24, 1814)
Hickey (1989), 'The War of 1812: A Forgotten Conflict'

events.certainty_note: The treaty date is precisely documented.

events.title: Congress of Vienna convenes; European territorial settlement begins
events.description: The Congress of Vienna opened in September 1814 (though the formal opening was delayed until November 1814) to reshape Europe after Napoleon's defeat. The major powers (Britain, Austria, Prussia, Russia, and France) negotiated the territorial settlement that would define European politics for the next century. The Congress was dominated by Klemens von Metternich (Austria), who sought to create a conservative balance of power to prevent future revolutionary upheaval. The Congress continued into 1815 and produced the Final Act in June 1815.

sources.name: Congress of Vienna protocols and preliminary documents
Schroeder (1994), 'The Transformation of European Politics 1763–1848'

events.certainty_note: The Congress opened in September 1814 (preliminary sessions) and formally in November 1814. The date is documented in diplomatic records.

events.title: Restoration of the Bourbon monarchy in France; Louis XVIII crowned
events.description: Following Napoleon's abdication on April 11, 1814, Louis XVIII, the Bourbon heir, was restored to the French throne. Louis XVIII was crowned on May 3, 1814, at the Cathedral of Reims. The Restoration marked the end of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic era and the beginning of a period of conservative reaction in Europe. Louis XVIII attempted to balance the interests of the old aristocracy with the new bourgeoisie and the legacy of the Revolution. He granted a constitutional charter (the Charter of 1814) that preserved some of the gains of the Revolution while restoring royal authority.

sources.name: Royal decrees and coronation records
Jardin & Tudesq (1973), 'Restoration and Reaction 1815–1848'

events.certainty_note: The restoration and coronation of Louis XVIII in 1814 are precisely documented in official records.

events.title: Battle of Toulouse; Marshal Soult defeats British and Spanish forces
events.description: The Battle of Toulouse was fought on April 10, 1814, between the French army under Marshal Nicolas Soult and the British and Spanish forces under the Duke of Wellington. Despite being outnumbered, Soult conducted a skillful defense and inflicted heavy casualties on the allied forces. However, the battle was ultimately inconclusive, and Soult was forced to withdraw. The battle was one of the last major engagements of the Peninsular War and occurred just days before Napoleon's abdication. It demonstrated the continued fighting capability of the French army even in the final days of the war.

sources.name: Military reports and eyewitness accounts from the battle
Oman (1930), 'A History of the Peninsular War'

events.certainty_note: The battle date (April 10, 1814) is precisely documented in military records.

events.title: Burning of Washington, D.C.; British raid during War of 1812
events.description: British forces raided Washington, D.C., on August 24–25, 1814, during the War of 1812. The British burned several government buildings, including the White House (then called the President's House) and the Capitol. The raid was a response to the American burning of York (modern Toronto) in 1813. The burning of Washington was a humiliating blow to American prestige but also galvanized American resistance. The raid demonstrated British military capability but did not lead to the conquest of American territory. The British withdrew after the raid, and the war continued until the Treaty of Ghent in December 1814.

sources.name: British military reports and eyewitness accounts
American accounts and newspaper reports
Hickey (1989), 'The War of 1812: A Forgotten Conflict'

events.certainty_note: The burning of Washington is precisely dated (August 24–25, 1814) from military records and eyewitness accounts.

events.title: Battle of Plattsburgh (Lake Champlain); American naval victory
events.region: North America (New York, near Lake Champlain)
events.description: The Battle of Plattsburgh (also called the Battle of Lake Champlain) was fought on September 11, 1814, between American and British forces during the War of 1812. The American naval squadron under Commodore Thomas Macdonough defeated the British squadron, forcing the British army to retreat. The American victory boosted morale and contributed to the British decision to seek peace. The battle demonstrated American naval capability and was one of the few clear American victories in the War of 1812.
key_figures: Captain George Downie (British commander, killed in battle)

sources.name: U.S. Navy records and Macdonough's reports
British military records
Hickey (1989), 'The War of 1812: A Forgotten Conflict'

events.certainty_note: The battle date (September 11, 1814) is precisely documented in naval records.

events.title: Latin American independence movements accelerate; Chile declares independence
events.description: The independence movements in Spanish America accelerated in 1814. In Chile, the independence movement was temporarily suppressed by Spanish forces, but the movement continued to gain momentum. Simón Bolívar was forced to flee Venezuela in 1814 but continued organizing the campaign for independence. José de San Martín was preparing to lead the liberation of Chile and Peru. The Spanish monarchy, weakened by the Peninsular War and the Napoleonic Wars, was unable to effectively suppress the independence movements. By 1814, most of Spanish America was in open rebellion or on the verge of it.
key_figures: José de San Martín (Argentine military leader)

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and independence movement documents
Lynch (1973), 'The Spanish American Revolutions 1808–1826'

events.certainty_note: The acceleration of independence movements in 1814 is well-documented in Spanish and Latin American sources.

events.title: Tokugawa shogunate issues edicts restricting foreign trade
events.description: The Tokugawa shogunate, under Shogun Tokugawa Ieyoshi, issued edicts in 1814 further restricting foreign trade and reinforcing the sakoku (closed country) policy. The edicts prohibited the construction of large ocean-going vessels and restricted foreign contact to the Dutch trading post at Nagasaki. This was part of the broader Tokugawa policy of isolationism that had been in place since the 17th century. The edicts reflected concerns about foreign influence and the stability of the shogunate's control.

sources.name: Tokugawa bakufu edicts and administrative records
Totman (1967), 'Politics in the Tokugawa Bakufu 1600–1843'

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Jiaqing Emperor consolidates power
events.description: The Jiaqing Emperor (r. 1796–1820) continued consolidating power in 1814, having suppressed the White Lotus Rebellion (1796–1804) and purged corrupt officials. The Qing Dynasty remained the dominant power in East Asia, but signs of decline were emerging: population pressure, agricultural stress, and the beginning of Western commercial pressure. The Jiaqing period is often seen as the beginning of the Qing's long decline, though the dynasty remained powerful and stable in 1814.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and edicts
Spence (1990), 'Search for Modern China'; Fairbank & Goldman (2006), 'China: A New History'

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Toulouse is sometimes portrayed as a French victory, but modern scholarship has noted that it was tactically inconclusive and that Soult was forced to withdraw. The burning of Washington is often depicted as a major British success, but it did not lead to the conquest of American territory and may have actually strengthened American resolve. Some historians have questioned the extent to which the War of 1812 was a 'draw' or whether it should be considered an American victory, given that the United States achieved its goal of preventing British conquest.
historiographic_note: 1814 is exceptionally well-documented for Europe and North America due to rich archival records, diplomatic correspondence, and military accounts. The Napoleonic Wars and their conclusion are among the most thoroughly documented events of the period. The Congress of Vienna and the restoration of the Bourbon monarchy are extensively documented in diplomatic records. However, sources for Latin America and other regions may be less complete in Western archives. The historical record for 1814 is among the richest for any year in this period.

graph_edges.note: Napoleon's abdication led to the restoration of Louis XVIII
Napoleon's defeat prompted the Congress of Vienna
Treaty of Ghent signed in 1814, ratified in 1815
British raid on Washington and American naval victory at Plattsburgh occurred in the same year
Latin American independence movements accelerated from 1814 into 1815
